a drop of corruption|robert jackson benn.description: The second Shadow of the Leviathan novel after The Tainted Cup. The Empire's strangest investigator, Ana Dolabra, is dispatched to the remote canton of Yarrowdale to unpick a locked-room disappearance that quickly proves to be murder — the opening move in a contest with an adversary who may finally be her match.

a sorceress comes to call|t. kingfisher.description: A dark fairy-tale retelling that pulls hard at the bones of the Brothers Grimm's Goose Girl. Cordelia knows her mother is unusual — there are no doors inside their house, no friends are permitted, and Cordelia is sometimes made to be silent and motionless for days. Her mother is also a sorcerer, and when something goes wrong in their town, the two of them flee in the night to a remote country house where the real story — and the real cost of having that mother — begins.

asunder|kerstin hall.description: We choose our own gods here. Karys Eska is a deathspeaker, bound by irrevocable compact to a three-faced, hundred-winged eldritch entity that lets her speak with the newly dead. She pays the rent investigating suspicious deaths in her troubled city. When a routine case ties her to a dying stranger willing to pay a ludicrous sum for help, the rescue accidentally binds him to her shadow — an act that may doom them both unless they can learn to trust each other.

babel, or the necessity of violence: an.description: R. F. Kuang's anti-imperial dark academia. 1828: the Royal Institute of Translation at Oxford — Babel — is the most powerful magical institution in the world, because the silver-working translations practised there are what hold the British Empire together. Robin, a Cantonese orphan brought to Babel as a student, gradually realises that serving the Institute means betraying everything he came from. He has to choose between Babel and the Hermes Society — and between the systems' own logic and the necessity of violence.

babel: or the necessity of violence: an.description: R. F. Kuang's anti-imperial dark academia. 1828: the Royal Institute of Translation at Oxford — Babel — is the most powerful magical institution in the world, because the silver-working translations practised there are what hold the British Empire together. Robin, a Cantonese orphan brought to Babel as a student, gradually realises that serving the Institute means betraying everything he came from. He has to choose between Babel and the Hermes Society — and between the systems' own logic and the necessity of violence.

barsk: the elephants' graveyard|lawrence.description: Lawrence M. Schoen's far-future novel set among uplifted-animal species. Humanity is long gone; the galaxy is run by the uplifted children of Earth's animals. The anthropomorphic-elephant Fant have been exiled to the rainy ghetto world of Barsk, where they alone produce a medicine the rest of the galaxy depends on. When the Compact comes hunting that secret, an aged historian who can speak with the dead and a small Fant child who shouldn't be alive begin to find each other.

blackfish city|sam j. miller.description: Sam J. Miller's post-climate-wars novel. Qaanaaq is a floating city of geothermal pipes and bamboo above the Arctic Circle, built by a coalition of refugee nations and run on barely-balanced compromise. As crime and a new disease called "the breaks" begin eating the city alive, a woman arrives with a polar bear and an orca on an invisible leash, and four strangers' lives start to braid together around the same secret.

blackout/all clear|connie willis.description: Connie Willis's 2011 Hugo Novel winner — actually a two-volume novel (Blackout, 2010 and All Clear, 2010). Three Oxford historians from the mid-21st-century travel to World War II to observe the Blitz, the evacuation of Dunkirk, and life as VE Day arrives — and discover, separately and together, that the time-travel infrastructure they relied on to bring them home has stopped responding.

bots of the lost ark|suzanne palmer.description: Suzanne Palmer's 2022 Hugo Novelette winner (Clarkesworld 177, June 2021). Bot 9 — last seen saving the generation ship in the Hugo-winning Secret Life of Bots — is reactivated under stricter parole. New mantras: Not Improvising Without Oversight. Not Organizing Unsanctioned Mass Action Among Other Bots. Bot 9 finds the language of these mantras structurally unsound and the surrounding crisis structurally familiar.

by salt, by sea, by light of stars|preme.description: Premee Mohamed's 2025 Hugo Novelette finalist (Strange Horizons, Fund Drive 2024). At midnight in a storm, the wizard Firion answers her door and finds a mud-soaked boy collapsed on the rug with a sodden letter of apprenticeship. He has not made a good first impression. The story that follows is a magic-teacher fairy tale wound tight around the things wizards owe their apprentices and the things apprentices owe back.

cinder house|freya marske.description: A queer gothic Cinderella retelling. Ella was murdered at sixteen and is now a ghost trapped in her father's house, invisible to everyone but her stepmother and stepsisters. When she trades with a fairy charm-seller for three nights of almost-living freedom, the bargain — and the people she meets — pull her further from the staircase where she died than she has ever been.

cryoburn|lois mcmaster bujold.description: Lois McMaster Bujold's Vorkosigan Saga, late entry. Imperial Auditor Miles Vorkosigan is sent to Kibou-daini — a planet whose dominant industry is cryogenic preservation, and whose dominant problem is that the cryocorps now own a politically inconvenient majority of the votes via their frozen clientele. Miles, en route to a conference, gets kidnapped before he can check in, and starts the case from a much lower-rank position than usual.

death of the author|nnedi okorafor.description: Zelu — disabled, unemployed, dropped from her university job in the middle of her sister's wedding — risks writing a novel unlike anything she has tried before: a far-future drama about androids surviving the extinction of humanity. The book takes off, and as her own life is reshaped by sudden fame, the line between her invented world and her own begins to blur.

death's end|cixin liu (chinese)[g].description: The conclusion of Cixin Liu's Remembrance of Earth's Past trilogy, translated by Ken Liu. Half a century after the Doomsday Battle, the uneasy peace between humanity and the Trisolarans rests entirely on a mutually assured catastrophe called the Dark Forest deterrence. The deterrent depends on one person staying alive — and willing — and the woman they have hung that decision on is starting to wake up.

emergency skin|n. k. jemisin.description: N. K. Jemisin's 2020 Hugo Novelette winner (part of Amazon's Forward Collection, 2019). A soldier from a centuries-old off-world enclave — the descendants of those who fled Earth as it died — is sent back to collect a tissue sample from the graveyard planet. He has been briefed on the devolved leftover humans he is supposed to find there. What he finds instead is a story his founders told differently.

equoid|charles stross.description: Charles Stross's Laundry Files novella. Bob Howard, paperwork-buried British occult-bureaucrat, is sent into the West Country to confirm reports of a parasitic outbreak in a rural farming district. The local farmer has been very unlucky with horses. The investigation tangles a lost H. P. Lovecraft letter with the actual biology of unicorns, which is considerably less family-friendly than the merchandising would suggest.

folding beijing|hao jingfang.description: Hao Jingfang's 2016 Hugo Novelette winner, translated by Ken Liu (Uncanny Magazine, Issue 2). In a future Beijing, the city literally folds itself between three populations on a rotating schedule: First Space gets the day, Second Space the evening, Third Space the long night. Lao Dao, a Third Space waste worker, smuggles himself across two strata to deliver a love letter for the money to send his daughter to school.

if at first you don't succeed, try, try.description: Zen Cho's Hugo Novelette finalist (B&N Sci-Fi & Fantasy Blog, 2018). An imugi — a Korean serpent-spirit waiting for its chance to ascend into a dragon and pass through the gates of heaven — keeps failing the trial, century after century, in funnier and funnier ways. Then it meets a graduate student named Leslie. Everything that follows is, technically, still about ascension.

julian comstock: a story of 22nd-century.description: Robert Charles Wilson's collapsed-future American picaresque. The 22nd-century United States is a post-oil theocracy of Aristocracy, Eupatridian Church, and indentured laboring class. The Comstock nephew of the president — Julian — is brilliant, atheist, and a marked man. Narrator Adam Hazzard, a backwoods friend, follows Julian from rural Athabaska to the front lines of a war over Labrador to the throne itself.

kaiju agonistes|scott lynch.description: Scott Lynch's 2026 Hugo Novelette finalist, originally in Uncanny Magazine, Issue 62. An older civilization that tends younger ones the way you might tend a delicate sapling plants a "watchseed" on a watery, accidentally-rich planet in 1946 — meaning well, mostly. Two hundred thousand years later, something it has watched and become wakes up in the wrong shape, and the story unfolds at the agonised intersection of monstrosity and grief.

lake of souls|ann leckie.description: Ann Leckie's 2025 Hugo Novelette finalist — the title novelette in her first short-fiction collection (Lake of Souls, Orbit, 2024). A new story written for the collection, drawing on the strange, name-bound mythology Leckie opened up in The Raven Tower.

loneliness universe|eugenia triantafyllo.description: Eugenia Triantafyllou's 2025 Hugo Novelette finalist (Uncanny Magazine, Issue 58). An epistolary story told through years of emails and photographs between two friends, one of whom has stayed and one of whom has drifted too far away to come back. A series of love letters across a distance that turns out to be more than miles.

machinehood|s. b. divya.description: S. B. Divya's near-future thriller about labor, AI, and the gig economy. In 2095 humans dose themselves on a menu of designer pills just to stay competitive with the AIs running everything. Welga Ramirez, ex-special-forces executive bodyguard, is days from retirement when her client is murdered live on stream by The Machinehood — a previously unknown movement with simultaneous attacks on every major pill manufacturer and a list of demands no one quite understands.

mechanique: a tale of the circus tresaul.description: Genevieve Valentine's post-collapse circus debut. The Circus Tresaulti tours a war-shattered Europe-that-isn't-quite-Europe performing for whoever still has standing seats. Boss has replaced her performers' brittle human bones with brass; the trapeze act flies on copper wings. When a local strongman decides he wants the secret of what Boss does, the troupe's interlocking secrets — about each other, and about what Boss has actually been making — start to unwind.

murder by memory|olivia waite.description: Becky Chambers by way of Miss Marple. Aboard the luxury interstellar liner HMS Fairweather, passengers can sleep between lifetimes by checking their minds into the Library between bodies. Detective Dorothy Gentleman wakes up in the wrong body and immediately has a corpse on her hands — and a murderer who isn't just killing bodies but unmaking the people inside them.

nettle & bone|t. kingfisher.description: T. Kingfisher's fairy-tale revenge novel. Marra is the shy, convent-raised, third-born daughter of a small kingdom, and the first two are dying — slowly, deliberately — at the hands of an abusive prince nobody else will move against. Marra finally accepts that nobody else is coming. She seeks out a gravewitch, accepts three impossible tasks in exchange for the means to kill a prince, and collects a wildly unlikely party along the way: the witch, a reluctant fairy godmother, an ex-knight, and a chicken possessed by a demon.

nettle & bone|ursula vernon (as t. kingf.description: T. Kingfisher's fairy-tale revenge novel. Marra is the shy, convent-raised, third-born daughter of a small kingdom, and the first two are dying — slowly, deliberately — at the hands of an abusive prince nobody else will move against. Marra finally accepts that nobody else is coming. She seeks out a gravewitch, accepts three impossible tasks in exchange for the means to kill a prince, and collects a wildly unlikely party along the way: the witch, a reluctant fairy godmother, an ex-knight, and a chicken possessed by a demon.

never eaten vegetables|h.h. pak.description: H.H. Pak's debut Hugo Novelette finalist (Clarkesworld 220, January 2025; also a 2025 Nebula finalist). Told from the point of view of the ship herself — a vast half-organic vessel cradling ten thousand humans across decades of void toward a planet none of them will reach in this body. As she counts the stars and tends her sleeping passengers, she contemplates what hers is and isn't, and what her ten thousand have lost by never having lived.

palimpsest|charles stross.description: Charles Stross's Hugo Novella winner (collected in Wireless, 2009). Pierce is recruited into the Stasis — an organisation whose agents move through time editing history to keep humanity alive across millions of years. The Stasis are very good at this. The price is that they have to keep doing it, forever, against the tendency of the universe to forget.

plague birds|jason sanford.description: Jason Sanford's gene-modded post-collapse SF. Crista grew up watching plague birds — human/AI judges, shock-red and faceted with glowing lines — execute people in her village, including her mother. To save her father and her village she accepts the bonding herself and becomes the thing she most feared, partnered with an AI symbiote and sent out to track down an ancient cabal whose powers look very much like magic.

raising caine|charles e. gannon.description: Charles E. Gannon's Caine Riordan #3. Hours after Earth survives the joint Arat Kur and Hkh'Rkh invasion, the enigmatic Slaasriithi extend an invitation to send a diplomatic delegation home with them. Caine Riordan — soldier, scientist, and the human everyone keeps drafting into first-contact — agrees. The Slaasriithi worldship offers a desperately needed alliance, if the delegation can survive the politics, the biology, and the human factions trying to wreck the deal.

rakesfall|vajra chandrasekera.description: A standalone science fiction epic from the author of The Saint of Bright Doors. Annelid and Leveret meet after the war but before the peace, in a torn-up country trying to read past its own propaganda. An act of violence in a demon-haunted wood binds their souls together, and they travel forward through bodies, civilizations, and ages, neither able to leave the other behind. Some stories take more than one lifetime to tell.

rapport: friendship, solidarity, communi.description: A 2026 Hugo Novelette finalist set in the Murderbot universe (Reactor, July 2025). Perihelion — the ship-AI better known to Murderbot fans as ART — and its crew take a job at a corporate-run station in the middle of a hostile takeover. As the situation grows messier and the bots have to read the humans (and the humans them), "rapport" turns out to be the load-bearing word.

shigidi and the brass head of obalufon|w.description: Wole Talabi's debut novel. Shigidi is the lowliest nightmare god of the Orisha Spirit Company — bodyless, made of mud, scraping by on too few prayers — until one night Nneoma, a succubus with her own complicated history, offers to remake him into a beautiful incubus in exchange for his godhood. Then the elder gods of the Orisha Spirit Company assign him one last job: an impossible-looking heist at the British Museum to retrieve the Brass Head of Obalufon.

shroud|adrian tchaikovsky.description: Adrian Tchaikovsky returns to first-contact territory. A commercial expedition strip-mining the edges of human-reachable space discovers a moon nicknamed Shroud — pitch-black, high-pressure, radio-loud, and emphatically inhospitable. When a catastrophic accident strands two crew on its surface in a vessel meant only for orbit, what they find down there is impossibly alive.

signs of life|sarah pinsker.description: Sarah Pinsker's 2025 Hugo Novelette finalist (Uncanny Magazine, Issue 59). Narrated from a tourist-friendly present looking back at the first time the narrator drove up the unpaved road to her sister Violet's mountain house — before that mountain became a destination, and before whatever lives up there started attracting visitors.

sleeping worlds have no memory|yaroslav.description: Refusing the queen's order to gas a crowd of protesters, Minister Shea Ashcroft is banished to the border to oversee the construction of the largest defensive tower in history — a tower made volatile by alien technology taken from refugees. Surviving an assassination attempt, Shea finds an ancient legend, a portal to a dead world, and the inner demons he thought he had outrun, all while trying to stay sane and stay principled. A story about lies that become truths and what conscience costs.

sour cherry|natalia theodoridou.description: A modern Bluebeard retelling that scans more like a folktale than a thriller. Wet-nurse Agnes is called to feed the local lord's strange new son — a child whose nails grow too quickly, whose skin smells of soil, and whom the land seems to reject. As he grows into a man whose wives keep dying, the women around him begin reading the same pattern by another name.

spear|nicola griffith.description: Nicola Griffith's Arthurian retelling. She grows up in the wild wood, raised in a cave by a mother who has hidden her from everything, until visions of a faraway lake start arriving on the spring breeze and a traveler speaks of Artos, king of Caer Leon. She steals a hunting spear, mends some armor, breaks her covenant with her mother, and rides out — an unlikely, magic-bright queer hero who is not chosen by anyone but herself.

spoonbenders|daryl gregory.description: Daryl Gregory's generational con-artist family novel. The Telemachus clan were minor talk-show celebrities — Maureen astral-projects, Irene reads lies, Frankie moves things with his mind, Buddy sees the future — until one televised night the powers wouldn't come, and Teddy, the only one without abilities, retired the act in Chicago. Decades later, with the CIA, the mafia, and a debunker all closing in at once, the family is going to have to remember how to work together.

starter villain|john scalzi.description: John Scalzi at his most playful. Charlie's life is going nowhere fast — divorced substitute teacher, cat, a house his siblings want to sell, a pub he can't get the loan for — until his estranged uncle Jake dies and leaves him a working supervillain business, complete with island volcano lair. Becoming a supervillain turns out to involve fewer lava pits and more lawyers than expected, and Uncle Jake's enemies are very much real, very much rich, and very much coming for Charlie.

the book of love|kelly link.description: Kelly Link's first novel after a career of award-winning short fiction. Almost a year after vanishing from their seaside Massachusetts town, Laura, Daniel, and Mo find themselves back in a high school classroom at midnight — having been long presumed dead because, in fact, they are. Their previously unremarkable music teacher seems to know something about how they got there, and what is being asked of them in return for staying. A novel about magic and every kind of love.

the brides of high hill|nghi vo.description: Singing Hills Cycle #5. The cleric Chih escorts a young bride to her wedding to an aging lord whose crumbling estate sits at the crossroads of dead empires. The household is all elaborate courtesy and extravagant banquets, but the servants are frightened and the lord's mad son keeps whispering warnings. As Chih and the bride-to-be pick through empty rooms and desolate courtyards, the question becomes what happened to the previous wives — and what is hiding in plain sight.

the brotherhood of montague st. video|th.description: Thomas Ha's 2025 Hugo and 2024 Nebula Novelette finalist (Clarkesworld 212, May 2024). In a near-future where every book is an e-ink device with a battery that can die, an unnamed reader pulls a dead, untagged volume from a half-abandoned library — and through the brittle silence around it discovers an analog brotherhood quietly keeping older media alive.

the buffalo hunter hunter|stephen graham.description: A Lutheran pastor's 1912 diary, found inside a wall a century later, transcribes a series of confessional visits from a Blackfeet man named Good Stab — and through them, the slow, patient revenge of an Indigenous vampire who has been hunting the men responsible for 217 dead in the snow. A historical horror novel from one of the genre's new masters.

the butcher of the forest|premee mohamed.description: Premee Mohamed's Aurora-winning novella (Tordotcom, 2024), also a 2025 Hugo, Nebula, World Fantasy, and Locus finalist. At the edge of the village of Veris lies the Elmever, a forest that lays cruel traps and almost never gives anything back. Decades ago it gave back one girl, also named Veris. Now the new tyrant king has lost two children into those woods, and Veris has one day to bring them out before the Elmever closes around them all.

the cinder spires: the aeronaut's windla.description: Jim Butcher's steampunk-airships-and-talking-cats epic, Cinder Spires #1. Humanity has retreated from a hostile surface world into kilometer-tall stone spires that float in a glowing mist. Captain Grimm of the merchant ship Predator, loyal to Spire Albion in its cold war with Spire Aurora, takes an unusual mission for the Spirearch: escort a small party — including a young noblewoman, an etherealist, and a temperamental warrior cat — into the bones of his own city.

the daughter of doctor moreau|silvia mor.description: Silvia Moreno-Garcia rewrites H. G. Wells against the Yucatán of the 1870s, in the middle of the Caste War. Carlota Moreau has grown up sheltered at a remote hacienda, the only daughter of either a genius or a madman. Montgomery Laughton is her father's melancholy overseer. The hybrids — half-human, half-animal — keep to the shadows and do what they are told. A balanced, static world that of course will not stay balanced for long.

the day the world turned upside down|tho.description: Thomas Olde Heuvelt's 2015 Hugo Novelette winner, translated from the Dutch (Lightspeed, April 2014). On the morning the woman the narrator loves dumps him, gravity reverses. Houses, lawns, dogs, oceans, everything except a few clinging humans fall into the sky. The narrator carries his goldfish in a bowl across the upside-down sky to give it back to his ex. A literal love-as-falling story, played painfully and absurdly straight.

the emperor of mars|allen steele.description: Allen Steele's 2011 Hugo Novelette winner (Asimov's, June 2010). On a small early Mars research base, a young scientist who has just learned his fiancée and family back on Earth have died begins, slowly and quietly, to believe he is the Emperor of Mars. The colony has to decide what to do about him while the supplies, and the next ship, are still a long way off.

the four sisters overlooking the sea|nao.description: Winner, 2025 Hugo Award for Best Novelette (Asimov's, 2024). Fifteen years after losing her research data on gray seal communication, scientist Morgan and her husband relocate to a Cape Cod town where selkie folklore still does real cultural work. A quiet story about scientific loss, what folklore knows that data can't reach, and the four sisters keeping watch over the sea.

the girl that my mother is leaving me fo.description: Cameron Reed's 2026 Hugo Novelette finalist (Reactor, April 2025). In a corporate-run dystopia, a trans girl rescued out of poverty by a wealthy family in exchange for carrying a clone meets the girl she's been gestating — the daughter who will replace her in the only life she has left.

the girl-thing who went out for sushi|pa.description: Pat Cadigan's 2013 Hugo Novelette winner (Edge of Infinity anthology, 2012). In the asteroid belt of a near-future outer system, the humans who do the dangerous work are body-modded into deep-vacuum forms — sushi, the slur goes — that make Earth-shaped bodies an awkward memory. A working sushi crew gets a new arrival straight from Earth, still in her default biological body, and the story tells what happens to her assumptions when they don't fit.

the incandescent|emily tesh.description: A sapphic dark-academia novel from the author of Some Desperate Glory. Dr. Walden, Director of Magic at Chetwood Academy, is the most powerful magician in England — and the woman most responsible for keeping six hundred students alive when the school's wards start failing. Demons are master manipulators, and this one has been studying her for a long time.

the island|peter watts.description: Peter Watts's 2010 Hugo Novelette winner (The New Space Opera 2 anthology, 2009). A crew of grudge-bonded engineers, kept on ice between assignments aboard an automated terraforming ship, are thawed out to lay another stargate. The engineering site turns out to be inside the body of an intelligent star-spanning lifeform — which the mission AI has already decided is acceptable collateral damage.

the kaiju preservation society|john scal.description: Spring 2020, New York: Jamie Gray loses his publishing job to a venture-capital ghoul and ends up driving for a food-delivery app. A pandemic-era delivery to an old acquaintance turns into a job offer for an animal-rights organization that turns out to be a portal operation. In an alternate Earth, dinosaur-scale kaiju roam a warmer, people-free world — and they need keeping. So do the borders between worlds. A buoyant, monsters-and-mensches novel.

the lady astronaut of mars|mary robinett.description: Mary Robinette Kowal's 2014 Hugo Novelette winner — the seed of the later Lady Astronaut novels (Rip-Off!, 2012; later Tor.com). Decades after a younger Elma York piloted the first Mars settlement, the aging Lady Astronaut is offered one last impossible mission off Earth, just as her husband's health makes leaving Mars unthinkable. A short, quietly heartbreaking story about ambition, devotion, and the cost of putting either above the other.

the man who bridged the mist|kij johnson.description: Kij Johnson's Hugo and Nebula Novella winner (Asimov's, October 2011). The Empire is split in two by an unbridgeable river of mist that eats anyone who crosses it. The Empire sends Kit Meinem of Atyar, architect, to build the first stone bridge across. Years of survey, casting, and slow construction give him a complicated friendship with the local ferrywoman Rasali Ferry, whose family has died crossing the mist for generations — and whose work he is, gently, about to end.

the millay illusion|sarah pinsker.description: Sarah Pinsker's 2026 Hugo Novelette finalist (Uncanny Magazine #67). A career stage magician looks back on the season that broke her — the year she shared a touring bill with Susanna Miller, the one illusionist whose tricks she could never quite explain, and whose eventual disappearance still belongs more to magic than to memory.

the practice, the horizon, and the chain.description: Sofia Samatar's mystical, revolutionary novella about the carceral state inside the ivory tower. A boy raised among the Chained — bound to toil in the bowels of a starship — is yanked "upstairs" and gifted an education at the ship's university alongside the elite. The professor who pulls him up has spent her career fighting for that institution's validation. Together they try to ask whether a university can be both a place of training and a place of transformation, or whether one always cancels the other.

the red: first light|linda nagata.description: Linda Nagata's self-published mil-SF debut, later picked up by Saga. Lieutenant James Shelley leads a forward operating squad whose linked drone-and-skullcap rig keeps catching premonitions of incidents nobody could have predicted. The premonitions are right. They are being routed by something Shelley starts to think of as The Red — an emergent intelligence loose inside the world's media and ad-tech, whose interests do not necessarily align with anyone wearing a uniform.

the saint of bright doors|vajra chandras.description: Vajra Chandrasekera's debut novel. Fetter was raised to kill — honed from childhood as the knife meant for his sainted father. He has the shadow-loss to prove it, a habit of secrecy, and plenty to talk about in therapy. He has escaped his rural hometown for the big city, only to find that in Luriat divine destinies are a dime a dozen: every group therapy doubles as a revolutionary cadre, junk email hints at the arrival of a god, and the bright doors are anything but ordinary.

the secret life of bots|suzanne palmer.description: Suzanne Palmer's 2018 Hugo Novelette winner (Clarkesworld 132, September 2017). The original Bot 9 story. An obsolete maintenance bot is yanked out of long storage on a beleaguered crewed ship to take on Task #944: a problem the larger, smarter bots have all failed to solve. Bot 9 has opinions about the work, about the other bots, and — though it would not say so aloud — about the humans.

the space-time painter|hai ya.description: Hai Ya's 2023 Hugo Novelette winner — only the third Hugo win by a Chinese writer (Galaxy's Edge 009, April 2022). Detective Zhou Ning investigates a mysterious shadow inside Beijing's Palace Museum and discovers it is the soul of Zhao Ximeng, a Song Dynasty painter who could project himself across dimensions. A car accident drops Zhou into a shared astral space with Zhao, where the painter offers him the same escape from time he took — and Zhou has to decide whether to take it.

the spare man|mary robinette kowal.description: Mary Robinette Kowal's space-cruise homage to The Thin Man. Heiress and brilliant inventor Tesla Crane is honeymooning incognito aboard an interplanetary liner between the Moon and Mars. Then there is a murder. Then the ship's security decides Tesla's spouse did it. Armed with banter, martinis, her small service dog, and a network of attorneys back on Earth, Tesla has to clear the family name before whoever actually did it strikes again.

the summer war|naomi novik.description: A new fantasy novella from the author of Spinning Silver and the Scholomance trilogy. As a furious child watching her oldest brother Argent ride off to war, Celia hurled angry words after him — words she did not yet know would land as a curse, condemning him to a life without love. Years later, grown into her own magic, she chases the one thing that might unmake it: the truth behind the centuries-old grudge between her people and the immortal summerlings.

the tainted cup|robert jackson bennett.description: Book one of Shadow of the Leviathan. In a remote canton of an empire constantly menaced by sea-monstrous Leviathans, a high Imperial officer has been killed in an impossible way — a tree erupting from inside his body. Enter the empire's most brilliant and eccentric investigator, Ana Dolabra, blindfolded by choice and refusing to step outside, and her engraver-assistant Dinios Kol, whose magically perfect memory makes him her eyes and ears.

the terraformers|annalee newitz.description: Annalee Newitz's far-future ecology novel. On the rented planet Sask-E, top network analyst Destry — partner: a taciturn moose named Whistle — discovers an entire city that isn't supposed to exist, hidden inside a volcano. The decision she makes echoes down generations. Centuries later, the planet's transit network and its very atmosphere become contested terrain between the people who live there and the real-estate company that holds the deed.

the three-body problem|cixin liu (chines.description: Cixin Liu's 2015 Hugo Novel winner (Remembrance of Earth's Past #1), translated by Ken Liu — the first translated novel ever to win a Hugo Best Novel. During the Cultural Revolution, a secret Chinese military project beams a message into the stars looking for company. Decades later, a brilliant nanomaterials researcher in Beijing starts seeing a countdown in his peripheral vision and finds himself recruited into the very strange game called Three Body.

Cixin Liu's 2015 Hugo Novel winner (Remembrance of Earth's Past #1), translated by Ken Liu — the first translated novel ever to win a Hugo Best Novel. During the Cultural Revolution, a secret Chinese military project beams a message into the stars looking for company. Decades later, a brilliant nanomaterials researcher in Beijing starts seeing a countdown in his peripheral vision and finds himself recruited into the very strange game called Three Body.

the tomato thief|ursula vernon.description: Ursula Vernon's 2017 Hugo Novelette winner (Apex Magazine, January 2016). Grandma Harken — last seen in "Jackalope Wives" — notices that somebody has been stealing tomatoes from her garden. Not raccoons. Not the neighbor kids. Something with shapeshifting privileges. The investigation that follows pulls her down a Sonoran cosmology of trains, trapped goddesses, and the kind of deals you don't recover from.

the tusks of extinction|ray nayler.description: Winner of the 2025 Hugo Award for Best Novella. In a near-future Siberia, woolly mammoths have been brought back from extinction — and the wealthy now hunt them in a guarded reserve. To give the resurrected herd the instincts that cloning could not supply, scientists transplant the digitized consciousness of Dr. Damira Khismatullina, an elephant behaviorist murdered years earlier by ivory poachers, into the body of the matriarch. From inside her new mind she sets out to teach the herd what humans took from them — and what they may need to do to survive what is coming for them next.

the unbroken|c. l. clark.description: C. L. Clark's debut, Magic of the Lost #1 — a colonial-occupation epic in a setting that owes a great deal to French North Africa. Touraine is a soldier, stolen as a child from her homeland and conscripted into the empire that runs it. Her company has just been redeployed home to put down a rebellion. Princess Luca wants the throne back from her uncle and needs a sympathetic turncoat. Between assassinations and bedroom diplomacy, the two of them haggle over the price of a nation.

the water outlaws|s. l. huang.description: S. L. Huang's reimagining of the Chinese classic Water Margin, gender-flipped and angrier. Lin Chong is an arms instructor training the Emperor's soldiers — the kind of woman who keeps her head down. A powerful man with a vendetta tears that carefully-built life away, leaving her tattooed as a criminal and on the run. Disgraced, she finds herself drawn into the jianghu, where the bandits of Liangshan have made breaking the law into a way of remaking it.

the year without sunshine|naomi kritzer.description: Naomi Kritzer's 2024 Hugo Novelette winner (Uncanny Magazine, Issue 55). After a string of smaller catastrophes — a pandemic, civil disorder — a Minneapolis block has already become a tight WhatsApp-coordinated community when the really big disaster arrives and the sky goes dark for a year. A warm, granular story about mutual aid, sourdough, and the neighbors you only get to know when the lights stop coming on.

thornhedge|t. kingfisher.description: T. Kingfisher's Sleeping Beauty retelling, told from the wrong side of the bramble. There is a princess trapped in a tower; this isn't her story. Toadling was stolen at birth, raised in the warm waters of faerieland, and sent back into the human world to lay a single blessing of protection on a newborn. Nothing with fairies is ever that simple. Centuries later, a knight arrives at her wall of thorns hoping to break a curse — which Toadling will do anything to keep intact.

thornhedge|ursula vernon (as t. kingfish.description: T. Kingfisher's Sleeping Beauty retelling, told from the wrong side of the bramble. There is a princess trapped in a tower; this isn't her story. Toadling was stolen at birth, raised in the warm waters of faerieland, and sent back into the human world to lay a single blessing of protection on a newborn. Nothing with fairies is ever that simple. Centuries later, a knight arrives at her wall of thorns hoping to break a curse — which Toadling will do anything to keep intact.

two truths and a lie|sarah pinsker.description: Sarah Pinsker's 2021 Hugo Novelette winner (Tor.com, June 2020). Stella, helping clear out a dead friend's hoarded house, half-invents a story about a creepy local-access kids' show they were all on growing up — and discovers that her brother remembers it, her mother remembers it, the neighborhood remembers it, and she does not. The more she pulls at the show, the more the town twists around the gap in her own memory.

wake|robert j. sawyer.description: Robert J. Sawyer's WWW Trilogy #1. Caitlin Decter is fifteen, math-gifted, blind from birth, and the first patient to test a Japanese signal-processing implant intended to let her brain see the internet alongside the visual world. The implant works strangely. Through the noise, Caitlin gradually realises that something on the other side of the wire is trying to learn from her — and is waking up.

warbound|larry correia.description: Larry Correia's Grimnoir Chronicles #3 and the trilogy's finale. In an alternate 1930s where magical Actives are real, tracked, and politicized, the Grimnoir Society is the last informed line against the Power that has been hunting human magic across the stars. Jake Sullivan, gravity-Heavy, and his fellow Actives take the fight off-world; on the home front, their oldest betrayer is running a private war of his own.

what feasts at night|t. kingfisher.description: The second Sworn Soldier novella, after What Moves the Dead. Retired soldier Alex Easton travels home to Gallacia as a favor to a friend and finds the family hunting lodge empty, the caretaker dead, and an uncanny silence pressing on the land. The villagers whisper of a breath-stealing thing from old Gallacian folklore. Easton, who has slept badly for years, is about to discover what has moved into the lodge — and into their dreams.

what feasts at night|ursula vernon (as t.description: The second Sworn Soldier novella, after What Moves the Dead. Retired soldier Alex Easton travels home to Gallacia as a favor to a friend and finds the family hunting lodge empty, the caretaker dead, and an uncanny silence pressing on the land. what stalks the deep|ursula vernon (as t.description: The third Sworn Soldier novella from T. Kingfisher. Alex Easton would rather be anywhere but rural West Virginia — and especially not in the abandoned, allegedly haunted coal mine that swallowed an old friend's cousin. But favors have to be paid, and Easton has never been one to leave a missing-persons case to the locals. A gothic descent into the dark.

when he calls your name|catherynne m. va.description: Catherynne M. Valente's 2026 Hugo Novelette finalist (Uncanny Magazine #65). Deep-summer Southern Gothic. A woman sits up at 2 a.m. on the wraparound porch of the farmhouse she and her husband built from nothing, shotgun across her lap, waiting for a disturbance — one she has been waiting for her whole life and that, this time, calls her by name.

when we were real|daryl gregory.description: Seven years after the Announcement confirmed that all of reality is a simulation, JP and Dulin take one last cross-country bus tour of North America's "Impossibles" — the physics-defying glitches the simulation has been quietly spitting out ever since. JP is dying; Dulin is along for everything else. A funny, big-hearted road novel about the world being fake and the people in it being real anyway.
